architecture.summary: This paper proposes a theoretical reinterpretation of transformer self-attention and residual streams through the lens of Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs). In this view, queries and keys define role-like subspaces, values encode fillers, scaled dot-product attention implements soft unbinding via similarity-weighted retrieval, and residual connections superpose multiple bound role-filler structures. The paper does not propose a new trained model but rather a conceptual framework connecting attention mechanisms to VSA algebra, with discussion of potential architectural biases (explicit binding/unbinding heads, hyperdimensional memory layers) and training objectives that would promote VSA-like behavior.

biblio.title: Attention as Binding: A Vector-Symbolic Perspective on Transformer Reasoning

claims.statement: Self-attention and residual streams implement an approximate Vector Symbolic Architecture: queries and keys define role spaces, values encode fillers, attention weights perform soft unbinding, and residual connections realize superposition of many bound structures.

Transformer-based language models display impressive reasoning-like behavior, yet remain brittle on tasks that require stable symbolic manipulation, such as variable confusion and inconsistency across logically related prompts.

Attention best approximates VSA binding when (i) role vectors (keys) are near-orthogonal to reduce interference, (ii) attention is relatively sparse, yielding crisp role-filler matches, and (iii) normalization ensures consistent geometric structure.
Chain-of-thought traces correspond to trajectories in a VSA-like state space where each reasoning step corresponds to adding or modifying bound role-filler pairs in the residual stream.

In real transformer models, the VSA interpretation is only approximate: learned embeddings and key projections are not perfectly orthogonal, heads often encode multiple functions simultaneously causing interference and imperfect unbinding, and attention patterns may be dense, blurring distinct bindings.

VSA-likeness can be measured through role-filler recoverability tests, interference under superposition, and alignment with VSA operators, evaluating whether attention heads approximate principled binding/unbinding or function as generic feature mixers.

Proposed VSA-inspired architectural biases include explicit binding/unbinding heads with multiplicative interactions and learned role vectors, and hyperdimensional memory layers maintaining superposed stores of bound role-filler vectors for storing logical constraints and intermediate results.

Training objectives can explicitly promote VSA-like behavior through orthogonality constraints on role vectors, auxiliary reconstruction tasks for unbinding, and logic-oriented auxiliary tasks such as permuted-variable reasoning and synthetic theorem-proving to encourage systematic generalization.
